items.description: Arabic numeral '7' in a small, plain later hand, positioned top right above the text block.
items.scholarly_note: Post-medieval pagination or foliation mark, added after the manuscript left Iceland (likely 17th–19th century during Danish royal collection or cataloguing); not original to the 13th-century mise-en-page.

items.description: Enlarged initial capital (resembling a flourished 'G' or 'Q') at the start of a new textual section in the lower half of the page, extending slightly left of the main text alignment with a modest descending flourish.
items.scholarly_note: Standard scribal device in Codex Regius to mark the opening of a new poem or major division (here within the Vafþrúðnismál/Grímnismál sequence). The form is functional rather than pictorial and belongs to the original campaign of the main hand.

items.description: Diffuse brownish discoloration and slight wear concentrated along the bottom edge and lower corners of the vellum.
items.scholarly_note: Age-related handling and environmental soiling typical of outer margins in composite codices; consistent with iron-gall ink chemistry and centuries of use. Does not impair legibility.

items.description: Very faint, shallow linear surface creases or natural parchment grain lines running across portions of the written area, visible primarily under close inspection of the vellum texture.
items.scholarly_note: Natural feature of the calfskin support or minor post-production handling abrasion; hair-side characteristics remain visible in places. No text is lost or obscured.

scratches: Faint linear creases following natural vellum grain within the text block and extending into lower margin
Minor surface abrasion consistent with hair-side preparation visible in select areas when viewed in raking light
damage_notes: Narrow margins with minor edge wear and cockling, especially lower and outer edges
No holes, perforations, tears, or medieval repairs visible on this leaf
No significant ink loss, flaking, or burn-through from iron-gall corrosion
Text block remains fully legible with consistent letter forms
summary: Page 16 of Codex Regius is a notably clean leaf containing a dense, professionally written block of Eddic verse in Gothic book hand with only minimal non-text additions. The principal features outside the regular script are a later-added pagination numeral '7' in the upper right and a conventional enlarged initial capital marking a sectional division. The vellum exhibits only minor age-related edge staining and natural textural variations; there are no doodles, pen trials, figurative marginalia (faces, animals), offset ink, interlinear corrections, or contemporary annotations visible. The mise-en-page is economical with narrow margins, reflecting the main scribe's efficient production practices circa 1270.
